pr_title: Add most recent Python versions in Travis CI
pr_body:
Add more recent Python versions including development branches and nightly build.

The motivation came from reading @brettcannon's article : https://snarky.ca/how-to-use-your-project-travis-to-help-test-python-itself/ . Trying to activate the newest Python versions on CI jobs is in most case a win-win situation: if everything works fine, there is nothing to worry about. If an issue is spotted, it is good to know about it to fix it on your side or to open a bug on CPython ( https://bugs.python.org/ ).

Also, if a failures is spotted, you can use the allow_failures option in your matrix build (more information about this in the link above).

Also, nightly and 3.7-dev may be a bit too recent/unstable so I can remove then if need be.

More information about how this PR happened on https://github.com/SylvainDe/CIthon .

#### What's this change do?
Implements the first bare features of BigIP clustering and scaling.

#### Any background context?
There is a need to cluster multiple BigIP devices (>=2 to <=8) with two basic cluster types ('sync-only', 'sync-failover'). This is the first basic features of that functionality. There are a few additional structures that have been added here. One important one is the f5/cluster directory, which holds the handlers for managing a cluster, device_group, and trusted_peers. These modules are simply wrappers around BigIP resource command (cm.device_groups.device_group) to control the order and timing of clustering.

Start at the single functional test for 'sync-failover' and then move on to the f5/cluster/__init__.py. This PR proposes to port https://github.com/apache/spark/commit/d7223bb9fdc54edcc1a45cead9a71b5bac49b2ab in PySpark into cloudpickle.

In short, `pickle.whichmodule` could fail, for example, in case of `six` - https://bitbucket.org/gutworth/six/issues/63/importing-six-breaks-pickling and it looks failed in `getattr`.

In such case, it looks nicer just to use `__main__` for the module name, which I believe we use when we can't find the module:

```python
>>> import pickle
>>> f = lambda x: x
>>> f.__module__ = None
>>> f.__name__ = ""
>>> pickle.whichmodule(f, f.__name__)
'__main__'
```

rather than throwing an exception. True, it is rather a band-aid fix but I believe this could be more beneficial in practice.

Please check out the details in https://bitbucket.org/gutworth/six/issues/63/importing-six-breaks-pickling and https://issues.apache.org/jira/browse/SPARK-16077

Fixes #105

Whenever a quantifier is triggered and a [new-match] line appears in the log we now additionally log explanations for all relevant equalities. These appear as steps that can be taken to reach the root of an equivalence class. In general these lines look like:
[eq-expl] <id of starting node> <step type (e.g. congruence)> <potentially other information like pairs of arguments that are equal for congruence steps> ; <target node>
The full documentation will be added to the wiki.

We do not expect performance differences (apart from maybe fewer enodes fitting into a single cache line due to an additional field and resetting the log status when updating the e-graph all changes are inside if-statements) when logging is turned off. With logging enabled there is in some cases a noticeable performance overhead compared to logging with the old version.

Extra preprocess code that is copied over can be removed once https://github.com/jupyter/nbconvert/pull/811 is merged.

Elf objects support a field that works similar to the LD_LIBRARY_PATH variable
embedded in the binary:

$ readelf -a ./main | grep RUNPATH
 0x000000000000001d (RUNPATH)            Library runpath: [/nix/store/sf3qh8ayxbhybmscanjrd3y3igrng304-env/lib64:/nix/store/sf3qh8ayxbhybmscanjrd3y3igrng304-env/lib:/nix/store/4h0allsz28x9mdirzb4wv51algacxzr7-glibc-2.26-131/lib:/nix/store/kzqrxkxs6lcbhxlalcim7zz6xay0i2f2-gcc-6.4.0-lib/lib]

DT_RPATH takes precedence over DT_RUNPATH and LD_LIBRARY_PATH, but is
ignored if DT_RUNPATH is set.
DT_RUNPATH comes after LD_LIBRARY_PATH.
The current implementation is incomplete as those flags can be also set
in shared libraries while here only the main object is take into
account.

After applying this patch it is possible to load such executables:

>>> import angr
>>> proj = angr.Project('./main', load_options={'except_missing_libs': True})
>>> print(proj.loader.all_objects)
[<ELF Object main, maps [0x400000:0x601017]>, <ELF Object libc-2.26.so, maps [0x1000000:0x13b18ff]>, <ELF Object ld-2.26.so, maps [0x2000000:0x222610f]>, <ELFTLSObject Object cle##tls, maps [0x3000000:0x300d010]>, <ExternObject Object cle##externs, maps [0x4000000:0x4008000]>, <KernelObject Object cle##kernel, maps [0x5000000:0x5008000]>]

This change comes at the moment without tests. 
I would like to hear your feedback on this feature first and how a test should look like
see discussion in #56

pr_title: Make scalar get_type an instance method.
pr_body:
Given the current way of how Scalars are mostly being created in the schema, e.g. `graphene.Boolean(description='Some boolean')` and then how they get 'magically' turned into a `Field` using `self.get_type()` it makes sense to make this an Instance method rather than a classmethod. This allows e.g. to set attributes on the instance that will always be accessible, as they are being returned by `get_type`.

This replaces https://github.com/graphql-python/graphene/pull/325

Further, if you are worried about backwards-compatibility we could also keep this a class method and then simply override that classmethod with an instancemethod on instantiation. Being a 100% backwards-compatible and allowing for the use-case described,

```
import types
...
class Scalar(six.with_metaclass(ScalarTypeMeta, UnmountedType)):
    '''
    Scalar Type Definition
    The leaf values of any request and input values to arguments are
    Scalars (or Enums) and are defined with a name and a series of functions
    used to parse input from ast or variables and to ensure validity.
    '''

     def  __init__(self):
        def get_type(self):
            return self
        self.get_type = types.MethodType(get_type, self)
  
    serialize = None
    parse_value = None
    parse_literal = None

    @classmethod
    def get_type(cls):
        return cls
```

pr_title: Stylize the newly selected node after expansion
pr_body:
Previously, when the user performs some functionality to expand the
graph and then select a node (such as navigating to a node after
observing TPU compatibility or clicking an op in the debugger plugin),
the graph would select a node before expanding its parent nodes.

This can cause an error because the selected node is not yet present in
the graph hierarchy:

```
(index):7546 Uncaught (in promise) TypeError: Cannot read property
'node' of undefined
   at HTMLElement._updateNodeState ((index):7546)
         at HTMLElement._selectedNodeChanged ((index):7547)
```

This change makes the scene only stylize the selected node
after expansion of necessary nodes finishes. I don't think I can repro
the issue after this change.

FYI, @caisq

# Description
In the main upload function we re-fetch objects (dataset and destination package) in order to verify that they are actual objects that exist on the platform. While this is good validation, performing a large number of uploads to the same destination and dataset requires needlessly making requests from the platform. Further, when the destination is collection, the API always responds with its constituent children, which, when continuously uploading to, can cause the time of that request to increase to the point where it may timeout.

Bug fix (non-breaking change which fixes an issue)

pr_title: Provide support for access_token refresh for flows (like OAuth2) that granted a refresh_token to a Connected App
pr_body:
``simple-salesforce`` seems like the most vibrant of the python Salesforce "SDK" modules, but it doesn't seem to inherently support the refreshing of access tokens when a Connected App (or perhaps other flow) was provided a ``refresh_token`` for this very purpose. I'd written my own wrapper class around ``simple-salesforce`` to accomplish this, but seemed like others would have this issue/problem as well so thought I should contribute. Salesforce's documentation is all over the place, but here's a decent, succinct example that illustrates what I'm adding to the ``simple-salesforce`` internals here: https://help.salesforce.com/apex/HTViewHelpDoc?id=remoteaccess_oauth_refresh_token_flow.htm&language=en

I tried to add/update decent comments and update the README, but feel free to change any formatting that I screwed up or provide any feedback you'd like me to incorporate.

Perhaps adding a "access_token_refreshed" callback that can be passed in by the user and executed when a token refresh is required and happens (successfully) would be a nice feature down the line, but starting with this.

pr_body: We're logging received messages at info level as well, and this is helpful for debugging stuff.

Expire mediated transfer pair when either unlock or withdraw failed to stop the error message from repeating.

If a channel is closed while we have a pending lock we correctly [detect](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/a025b2f5b56888ce327bf1cb4d2ee5e16d7fc657/raiden/transfer/mediated_transfer/mediator.py#L393) it in the state machine [generate](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/a025b2f5b56888ce327bf1cb4d2ee5e16d7fc657/raiden/transfer/mediated_transfer/mediator.py#L402) an event and then at the event handler we log an [error](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/a025b2f5b56888ce327bf1cb4d2ee5e16d7fc657/raiden/event_handler.py#L190).

That is because in each round the transfer appears as pending because only one of the two parts of the pair is set to a [final state](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/a025b2f5b56888ce327bf1cb4d2ee5e16d7fc657/raiden/transfer/mediated_transfer/mediator.py#L145).

This PR just sets both parts to a final state in an attempt to have the error logged only once and not for every block.

pr_title: Speed up startup by deferring imports

It's better for startup time since fewer files are touched on the hard drive.
It's better for missing dependencies - they will not trigger an exception if they are not needed.
It's worse for missing dependencies - exception will not be raised immediately, but only when check is executed.

links to issue #45

These are just some trivial changes that hopefully make the code
slightly more pleasant to work with.

The default GraphQL formatted is a bit human-unfriendly:
- It uses 2-space indents, which makes it difficult to work with in a 4-space Python environment.
- Since directives have no required parameter order, `@filter` gets its parameters printed backwards. That makes the operator the last parameter, and means that `@filter` is in hard-to-read postfix notation.

This fixes both those complaints.

pr_title: Schema Loader Generic Errors to Specific Schema Errors
pr_body:
#### Pull Request Checklist:
- [x] Tests for the changes have been added
- [ ] Docs have been added / updated

### What kind of change does this PR introduce?
The Schema Loader used `GenericSchemaError` at several places that did not qualify the kinds of error that were happening.

- Created new error types for schema loads / spec loads / store loads
- Errors are not raised immediately, and instead are added to the error collection
- Found a way to break dependency of WindowDTC on StreamingDTC - validation can now work on isolated files like they used to.
GlobalMaxPooll -> GlobalMaxPool

Pretty much adds a spec for a fixed stacked RNN that can be supported by CuDNN backend. Attributes are taken almost directly from CuDNN/TensorRT spec.

One interesting question is how to standardize the layout of parameters. Theoretically, layout of parameters in CuDNN is opaque (and one needs to set them via separate calls), however we need to choose some. I happen to pick the layout of parameters that corresponds to the real one used by CuDNN (did some reverse engineering). Backend implementations should still go with cudnn calls to reorder the parameters, but it might be convenient for some early testing.

Alternative to merging all parameters in a single tensor would be having directions * layers * 2 * 4 * 2 separate tensors which would be too much in my opinion.

When we land the generic RNN support in ONNX, I plan to add a standard translator from the described format to individual tensors that can be fed into generic RNN operators. That translator can also handle the weights reordering.

Reference cudnn wrappers in frameworks:
- Caffe2: https://github.com/caffe2/caffe2/blob/master/caffe2/operators/recurrent_op_cudnn.h
- PyTorch:
https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/master/torch/nn/modules/rnn.py
https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/ceb4f84d12304d03a6a46693e54390869c0c208e/torch/nn/_functions/rnn.py#L254
https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/625850c2c2f28c67881dc24f5d3abb80fd6e8483/torch/backends/cudnn/__init__.py#L40
https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/master/torch/backends/cudnn/rnn.py
- CNTK: https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/cognitive-toolkit/OptimizedRNNStack
- mxnet: https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/master/src/operator/cudnn_rnn-inl.h
